Implementing Packing Codes

Implementing Packing Codes

It is important to map connector-level packing codes to the appropriate storage format whenever possible. The Uniface data type associated with the packing code must correspond to the data type associated with the DBMS storage format.

For example, a Date data type in Uniface (type D) should always end up as a Date in the DBMS, if possible. The data types used by Uniface are generally supported by DBMSs, so this should not be a problem.

To implement packing codes in your connector:

  1. Identify a set of connector-level packing codes that correspond directly (without format conversions) to the DBMS storage formats.
  2. Redefine every connector-level packing code that does not correspond to a DBMS storage format to a packing code found in the first step.
  3. Implement the connector to support the packing codes found in the first step.

If you map a packing code to an inappropriate storage format, you must take the following problems into account:

  • Non-Uniface tools cannot understand the contents of fields with that storage format.
  • The DBMS is not capable of performing semantically correct operations on the field, such as ordering, selecting and performing aggregate computations.
  • DBMS indexes may not sort correctly. The ordering implied by an index is based upon the interpretation of the fields in the index by the DBMS. If an inappropriate mapping causes incorrect ordering by the DBMS, the connector service function uready does not work correctly. For example, numbers stored as ASCII are not sorted correctly.

As an example of an inappropriate mapping of packing codes to storage formats, consider a DBMS which has no Date data type. The connector can solve this by mapping all packing codes associated with the Date data type to the D1 packing code (ASCII date), and treat fields with the D1 packing code as strings. In this case, a non-Uniface tool will not be capable of interpreting the string field as a Date (unless that tool happens to use the same string representation for Dates). Furthermore, the DBMS cannot correctly compute a maximum date, for example, because it does not interpret the string field as Date.

The connector can solve this last problem by delegating selections, aggregate computations, and so on, to Uniface, thereby effectively treating the DBMS as a record management system where Date fields are concerned. This would significantly increase the complexity of the connector.

Related Links
  • Packing Code to DBMS Storage Format Mappings

    Uniface automatically maps model-level packing codes to connector-level packing codes, so the connector only needs to be aware of the connector-level packing codes.

  • uready

    Informs the connector whether it needs to continue searching through the preferred index.
